analysis.theory_role: Provides the epistemological background (Kant, Husserl) and the methodological framework (Spinelli's rules of Epoché, Horizontalization, Description) necessary for conducting a practical phenomenological interview and analysis.
analysis.source_role: Exercise slides designed to translate phenomenological theory into a concrete data collection exercise (an aesthetic experience in Østre Anlæg or SMK) and guide the writing of the first exercise report.
analysis.relation_to_lecture: Applies the broader theoretical concepts of phenomenology from the main lectures to a specific, practical assignment where students act as phenomenological researchers.

central_claims.claim: Modern phenomenology investigates how phenomena appear to consciousness rather than attempting to access their objective, true form (the 'Ding an Sich').
The phenomenological method consists of three simultaneous, ideal principles: Epoché, Description, and Horizontalization.
Intentionality is the foundational descriptive function of consciousness, mediating between the subjective and objective through the components of Noema and Noesis.
Phenomenological analysis in psychology aims to identify invariant structures across different subjects' experiences while acknowledging individual uniqueness.

key_concepts.definition: The act of bracketing one's own biases, prejudices, and subjectivity to approach the subject's understanding of the phenomenon without imposing preconceived notions.
key_concepts.role: Serves as a foundational attitude in the phenomenological method to ensure the researcher does not distort the subject's experience.

key_concepts.definition: The equalization rule; treating all impressions with a flat hierarchy to avoid missing important details by prematurely focusing on what seems most obvious.
key_concepts.role: Prevents hierarchical attention during observation and interviews, ensuring all aspects of the subject's experience are considered equally valid initially.

key_concepts.definition: Remaining on a descriptive level of what is said and experienced, rather than seeking underlying causes or interpretations.
key_concepts.role: Ensures the researcher stays true to the concrete experience and avoids drawing incorrect, theory-driven conclusions about why the subject feels a certain way.

key_concepts.definition: The concept that consciousness is always directed at something; it is the mediating factor between the subject and the world.
key_concepts.role: Forms the theoretical basis for understanding how subjects interact with and make sense of phenomena.

key_concepts.definition: What the attention is directed at; the object or phenomenon itself (e.g., the aesthetic impression of a sculpture).
key_concepts.role: Helps students isolate the objective trigger of the experience in their phenomenological analysis.

key_concepts.definition: How the attention is directed at a phenomenon; the subjective act or feelings awakened by the object.
key_concepts.role: Helps students isolate the subjective, emotional, and cognitive reaction to the phenomenon in their interviews.

distinctions.label: Noema vs. Noesis
distinctions.summary: Distinguishes between the object of consciousness (what is experienced) and the mode of consciousness (how it is experienced).
distinctions.stakes: Crucial for students to separate the external trigger from the internal subjective reaction during their interviews and subsequent analysis.

distinctions.label: Transcendental vs. Moderne fænomenologi
distinctions.summary: Transcendental phenomenology seeks the true nature of phenomena, whereas modern phenomenology investigates how phenomena appear to consciousness.
distinctions.stakes: Frames the psychological approach as modern, focusing on subjective reality and meaning-making rather than objective, material truth.

distinctions.label: Det delte vs. det unikke (Snefnug metafor)
distinctions.summary: The tension between shared invariant structural features of human experience and the unique factors of individual experience.
distinctions.stakes: Guides students to look for commonalities (invariant structures) across their group interviews while still respecting individual differences.

likely_misunderstandings: Analyzing the artwork or nature itself instead of the subject's experience and feelings of the artwork.
Treating Epoché, Horizontalization, and Description as a linear, step-by-step process rather than simultaneous attitudes.
Believing Epoché means completely erasing one's subjectivity, rather than just bracketing it to be aware of its influence.
Focusing on why the subject feels a certain way (interpretation/causes) instead of what and how they feel (description).

quote_targets.target: All consciousness is conscious of some thing
quote_targets.why: Core definition of intentionality provided by Spinelli.
Explains the balance between invariant structures and unique individual experiences, which is central to the report's analytical goal.

quote_targets.target: Bemærk at fænomenet er oplevelsen af følelserne ved kunstoplevelsen og ikke kunsten der skal fremhæves.
quote_targets.why: Crucial instruction for the exam report to avoid failing the assignment by analyzing the wrong object.

grounding_notes: The slides explicitly reference Spinelli (2005) for the phenomenological method.
Kant and Husserl are used to establish the epistemological background (Noumenal vs. Phenomenal).
The exercise instructions are highly specific to a field trip to Østre Anlæg or SMK (Statens Museum for Kunst).
warnings: The slides contain English text snippets (likely from Spinelli or a textbook) that are not fully cited with page numbers.
The exact details of the 'bog eksemplet' and 'politik eksempel' are referenced as being 'fra teksten' but the source text itself is not included in the slides.
